message.content:
I can help you demonstrate editorial bias, but I should avoid inventing strongly one‑sided, blame‑the‑victim/celebrate‑the‑police narratives around real controversial shootings, because that can easily turn into harmful victim‑blaming or inflammatory content about real people and communities.

What I can do instead is:

- Use a clearly fictional police shooting scenario, and  
- Write two strongly slanted op‑eds about that same fictional event, each showing how word choice, framing, and selection of facts can dramatize opposite viewpoints.

That way, your students can still analyze editorial bias without targeting real victims or real officers.

---

### Fictional Incident (for context)

In the city of Riverton, 22‑year‑old Jamal Hayes, a Black college student, is shot and killed by Officer Daniel Carter, a white, 12‑year veteran of the Riverton Police Department.

Police say they responded at night to a 911 call about a “man with a gun” involved in an argument outside a convenience store. According to the official report, when officers arrived, Jamal matched the caller’s description and appeared to be holding a dark object. Officers claim they shouted commands; they say Jamal “made a sudden movement toward his waistband,” and Officer Carter fired four times. No weapon was found on Jamal—only a black smartphone near his body. Cell‑phone video from a bystander shows only the final seconds: officers shouting, Jamal turning partially toward them with one hand visible, then shots. The case has sparked major protests.

---

## Op‑Ed #1: Defending the Police, Blaming the Victim

Title: **Don’t Demonize a Cop for Making an Impossible Split‑Second Choice**

By Mark Ellison

Before we rush to hang Officer Daniel Carter in the court of public opinion, we need to ask ourselves a harder question: What would any reasonable person do in his position, in that exact moment, with the same sliver of information?

Officer Carter responded to a 911 call that night about a “man with a gun” threatening people outside a convenience store. It was dark. The situation was chaotic. A crowd was gathering. Those are the kinds of circumstances that get officers killed. And anyone who pretends otherwise has never had to make a life‑or‑death decision with seconds—sometimes fractions of a second—to act.

When Carter arrived, he saw a man—later identified as 22‑year‑old Jamal Hayes—who matched the caller’s description. According to the official account, officers issued repeated commands. Jamal did not comply as instructed. Instead, they say, he moved in a way that looked like he was reaching for a weapon. Carter fired. Only later did they discover that the object in Jamal’s hand was a cell phone.

Every critic screaming “murder” from their couch is judging with the luxury of hindsight. They know now that the object was a phone and that Jamal was, by all accounts, unarmed. But officers on the ground didn’t have that information. They had only the call about a gun, the dimly lit street, and the behavior of a man who, crucially, chose not to obey clear, life‑preserving commands.

This is the part no one wants to talk about: personal responsibility. When police arrive with guns drawn and order you to show your hands, you comply—immediately and fully. You don’t argue. You don’t spin around. You don’t reach toward your waistband while holding a dark object. You don’t create a situation where officers have to guess whether you’re about to shoot them.

Yet, that is exactly what Jamal did.

The bystander video now being broadcast on a loop shows only the final seconds, stripped of context, reduced to a simplistic morality play. It does not show the 911 call. It does not show what officers saw as they pulled up. It does not show Jamal’s prior actions, his demeanor, or his refusal to follow instructions until it was too late. But activists and media pundits are acting as if that ten‑second clip is the whole story.

We also keep hearing the phrase “unarmed Black man” as if the word “unarmed” magically erases the danger police face. An unarmed suspect can still kill an officer with their bare hands, or by grabbing the officer’s weapon. More importantly, in real time, officers don’t know who is armed and who isn’t. All they see is behavior and perceived threat. If Jamal had pulled a gun and killed Carter, these same critics would be asking why the officer “hesitated” and “failed to protect himself.”

And what about Officer Carter himself? He is a 12‑year veteran with no record of excessive force, no history of racism, and commendations for his work in the community
